entries.title: What is type juggling in PHP
entries.body:
PHP is a weakly typed language: it tries to guess the programmer's intent and automatically coerces variables to different types when it considers it necessary. For example, a string containing only digits can be treated as an int or a float.

This automatic coercion (type juggling) produces unexpected results, especially in comparisons using ==, which checks only equality of value (loose comparison), not equality of type AND value (strict comparison).

Where to look: any code where the attacker controls one of the compared variables - password/token/HMAC checks, authorization and authentication bypass.

entries.title: == vs === - loose and strict comparison
entries.body:
Loose comparison: == or != - both variables have "the same value".
Strict comparison: === or !== - both variables have "the same type AND the same value".

A type juggling vulnerability occurs when loose comparison (== or !=) is used instead of strict (=== or !==) in a place where the attacker controls one of the compared variables. This can force the application to return an incorrect true/false answer and lead to serious authorization and/or authentication bugs.

entries.title: True Statements - expressions that yield true with ==
Table representing the behavior of PHP with loose comparisons (==)

meta.caption: A matrix of results of the == operator in PHP for pairs of values (TRUE/FALSE, numbers, "0e1", "0e99", "2abc", NULL, [], {}, etc.). Pairs that yield TRUE are highlighted in gray. Source: PayloadsAllTheThings.

entries.title: NULL Statements - md5/sha1 of an array return NULL
Loose comparisons exist not only in PHP
entries.body:
Loose type coercions in comparisons are found in many languages/DBMS, not just PHP. Ready-made Loose-Compare-Tables (Hakumarachi) exist for:

MariaDB, MySQL, NodeJS, PHP, Perl, Postgres, Python, SQLite.

Repository: github.com/Hakumarachi/Loose-Compare-Tables

entries.title: PHP 8 - type juggling when comparing strings with numbers is almost dead
entries.body:
In PHP 8 a string is no longer coerced to a number automatically (RFC "Saner string to number comparisons") - collisions with hashes starting with 0e and similar are finally a thing of the past.

The RFC "Consistent type errors for internal functions" removes bypasses of the form 0 == strcmp($_GET['username'], $password): strcmp no longer returns null with a warning, but throws a proper exception.

Bottom line: magic hashes and strcmp tricks work on PHP <= 7, but not on PHP 8.

entries.title: What are magic hashes (0e...)
entries.body:
Magic hashes arise from a quirk of type juggling in PHP when comparing a string hash with a number. If the hash string starts with "0e" followed only by digits, PHP interprets it as scientific notation (0 to the power of N = 0) and in a comparison treats it as float 0.

Consequence: two different passwords whose md5/sha1 both have the form 0e[digits only] will, under loose comparison of hashes (==), turn out to be "equal" to zero and to each other. This breaks password/token checks based on == instead of ===.

entries.title: Table of magic hashes MD4 / MD5
Table of magic hashes SHA1 / SHA-224 / SHA-256

rows: Michael A. Cleverly, Michele Spagnuolo & Rogdham

entries.title: Magic hash collisions (PHP) - var_dump
entries.body:
<?php
var_dump(md5('240610708') == md5('QNKCDZO')); # bool(true)
var_dump(md5('aabg7XSs')  == md5('aabC9RqS'));
var_dump(sha1('aaroZmOk') == sha1('aaK1STfY'));
var_dump(sha1('aaO8zKZF') == sha1('aa3OFF9m'));
?>

entries.title: Vulnerable code - loose comparison of HMAC from cookie
Logic of HMAC bypass via a 0e hash
entries.body:
The vulnerability is in the loose comparison (!=) by which $cookie['hmac'] is checked against the computed $hash.

If the attacker controls $cookie['hmac'] and sets it to the string "0", and also gets hash_hmac to return a hash of the form "0e" + digits only (treated as 0), then the condition $cookie['hmac'] != $hash becomes false - the HMAC check is bypassed.

We control 3 elements of the cookie:
• $username   - who we are impersonating, usually "admin"
• $expiration - a UNIX timestamp, must be in the future
• $hmac       - the supplied hash, "0"

Exploitation:
1. Prepare the cookie: username = "admin", expiration = a future timestamp, hmac = "0".
2. Brute-force $expiration until hash_hmac produces a hash starting with "0e" followed only by digits (a "zero-like" hash). This is expensive in CPU and not always feasible, but on success gives a bypass.
3. Substitute the found expiration value into the cookie. In the example the key was assumed to be empty: $key = '';

entries.title: Brute-forcing expiration to a 0e hash + final cookie
